quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 6 Quiz 9
quiz.subtitle: Interference
quiz.subject: Physics
quiz.topic: Grade 10 - Waves and Simple Harmonic Motion

questions.question: When two waves meet at a point, the resultant displacement is the sum of the individual displacements. This is called the:
questions.explanation: The principle of superposition states that when waves overlap, their displacements add together.

questions.question: Constructive interference occurs when two waves meet:
options: At a boundary between two media
At a gap in a barrier
questions.explanation: Constructive interference happens when waves are in phase — crests align with crests, amplifying the displacement.

questions.question: Destructive interference occurs when two waves meet:
questions.explanation: Destructive interference happens when waves are out of phase — crests meet troughs, reducing or canceling displacement.

questions.question: When two identical waves interfere constructively, the amplitude of the resulting wave is:
options: Equal to one of the original waves
The sum of the two amplitudes
The difference of the two amplitudes
questions.explanation: Constructive interference adds the amplitudes together. Two identical waves produce a wave with double the amplitude.

questions.question: When two identical waves interfere destructively (equal amplitude, completely out of phase), the resulting amplitude is:
questions.explanation: Complete destructive interference between waves of equal amplitude results in cancellation — zero amplitude.

questions.question: In a standing wave, points that remain permanently at rest (zero displacement) are called:
questions.explanation: Nodes are points of zero displacement in a standing wave, where destructive interference is complete at all times.

questions.question: In a standing wave, points of maximum displacement are called:
questions.explanation: Antinodes are points of maximum displacement where constructive interference occurs between the traveling waves.

questions.question: A standing wave is formed by:
options: Two identical waves traveling in the same directions
Two identical waves traveling in opposite directions
Two different waves traveling at different frequencies
A single progressive wave diffracting around an obstacle
questions.explanation: Standing waves result from the superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions, typically from a wave and its reflection.

questions.question: Noise-canceling headphones work by producing sound waves that:
options: Have higher frequency than the noise
Are in phase with the noise
Are out of phase with the noise
Reflect the noise away from the ear
questions.explanation: By generating sound waves out of phase with ambient noise, the headphones create destructive interference, canceling the unwanted sound.

questions.question: For two coherent sources, constructive interference occurs when the path difference is:
options: An odd number of half wavelengths
questions.explanation: Constructive interference occurs when waves arrive in phase — the path difference is $0, \lambda, 2\lambda$, etc.

questions.question: For two coherent sources, destructive interference occurs when the path difference is:
options: An odd number of half wavelengths
questions.explanation: Destructive interference occurs when waves arrive out of phase — path difference is $\lambda/2, 3\lambda/2, 5\lambda/2$, etc.

questions.question: Two waves have a wavelength of 2 m. At a point where the path difference is 4 m, what type of interference occurs?
questions.explanation: Constructive interference occurs when the path difference is a whole number multiple of the wavelength. Since 4 m = 2 × 2 m, the waves arrive in phase and produce constructive interference.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered interference and standing waves!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between constructive and destructive interference conditions.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on path difference, standing wave terminology, and real-world applications.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: superposition, constructive vs. destructive interference, nodes and antinodes.
